name: Color Matching
description: The player aligns colored channels, symbols, lights, or objects so the current pattern matches the required solution.
design_notes: Color matching puzzles need color_channel_id data plus a non-color representation so the solution is not hidden from color-blind players. State should be compared through solution_state_hash or channel mappings, and reset or hint behavior should preserve the player's latest understandable progress.
design_purpose: Create readable categorization puzzles using color, symbol, shape, or channel alignment.
Support lock codes, light routing, item sorting, and pattern completion in compact spaces.
Give designers a puzzle form that can scale from simple matching to multi-channel logic.
player_fantasy: The player feels observant when they notice the pattern and align the right channels.

parameters.name: puzzle_id
parameters.description: Stable puzzle identifier used by state, hints, and save data.

parameters.name: color_channel_id
parameters.description: Channel or symbol identifier that participates in the matching rule.

parameters.name: channel_assignment_map
parameters.typical_range: slot ids mapped to channel ids
parameters.description: Current player-authored assignment for slots, receivers, or objects.

parameters.name: solution_state_hash
parameters.description: Canonical solved arrangement used by validation and authoring tests.

parameters.name: hint_stage_id
parameters.description: Assistance stage that reveals pattern structure without requiring color perception.

edge_cases: Two colors are visually similar under lighting, post-processing, or colorblind display modes.
A channel changes during animation while validation reads the previous assignment.
The puzzle is solved by symbols but the UI still reports only color names.
Save/load restores object positions but not the current channel assignment map.
common_bugs: Validation compares display color values instead of stable color_channel_id identifiers.
Hint state reveals an old solution after designers reorder channels in content data.
A solved state fires repeatedly when players continue rotating already-correct pieces.
Colorblind mode changes the palette but not particle, icon, or prompt colors.
balancing_notes: A good color puzzle should be solvable from structure, symbols, or placement, not color alone.
More channels increase difficulty quickly; use grouping or intermediate feedback for larger sets.
Randomized channel assignments need reproducible seeds if the puzzle appears in guides, dailies, or multiplayer.
accessibility_notes: Always pair color with icons, labels, shapes, patterns, or placement cues.
Offer high-contrast and colorblind-safe palettes for puzzle-critical channels.

implementation_notes.unity: Store channel assignments as identifiers in a puzzle component and derive materials, icons, and lights from those ids. Use solution_state_hash for validation, and keep accessibility palette swaps separate from the solved-state data.
implementation_notes.godot: Use Resources for channel definitions and a puzzle node for current assignment. Controls or interactables should submit channel ids to one validator, while shaders and sprites render the selected accessibility palette.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use Gameplay Tags or data assets for channel ids, with replicated puzzle state if co-op players can manipulate pieces. Material parameter changes should observe committed state rather than being the source of validation.
implementation_notes.web: Keep puzzle state as channel ids and render CSS/canvas colors from a palette table. Store symbol labels alongside colors so the same model supports colorblind modes and text-based hints.

example_games.title: The Witness
example_games.note: Uses color and symbol logic in several puzzle rule sets.

example_games.title: Portal 2
example_games.note: Uses colored gels and clear channel affordances in spatial puzzles.
